en.extract:
The Venezuelan bristle tyrant (Pogonotriccus venezuelanus) is a Near Threatened species of passerine bird in the family Tyrannidae, the tyrant flycatchers. It is endemic to Venezuela.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Venezuelan bristle tyrant was originally described as Pogonotriccus venezuelanus. Genus Pogonotriccus was later merged into genus Phylloscartes. Beginning in 2016 taxonomic systems resurrected Pogonotriccus for this species and a few others.
The Venezuelan bristle tyrant is monotypic.


Description
The Venezuelan bristle tyrant is about 11.5 cm (4.5 in) long and weighs 8 to 9 g (0.28 to 0.32 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a gray crown, yellowish white lores, and a prominent yellowish white eye-ring on an otherwise mottled yellowish face. Their ear coverts have a blackish crescent at the top and rear. Their back and rump are olive. Their wings are blackish with pale yellow edges on the flight feathers. Their wing coverts are blackish with pale yellow tips that show as two wing bars. Their tail is olive. Their throat and underparts are bright yellow with a very light olive wash on the breast. Both sexes have a brown iris, a long, pointed, blackish bill with a whitish or pinkish base to the mandible, and gray legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The Venezuelan bristle tyrant has two separate ranges in Venezuela. On is in the coastal mountains from Carabobo through Aragua into the Federal District. The other is in the interior mountains of southern Aragua and Miranda states. The species inhabits humid montane forest where it favors mid-elevation cloudforest heavy with mosses. In elevation it ranges between 950 and 1,400 m (3,100 and 4,600 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The Venezuelan bristle tyrant is a year-round resident throughout its range.


Feeding
The Venezuelan bristle tyrant feeds on arthropods. It forages in the forest's mid-story up into the lower canopy, typically perching upright and making short upward sallies to snatch or hover-glean prey from vegetation. It usually forages singly or in pairs and usually as part of a mixed-species feeding flock.


Breeding
The Venezuelan bristle tyrant is thought to breed between January and April or May. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.


Vocalization
The Venezuelan bristle tyrant's song is "a fast trill, ch'e'e'e'e'd'd'd'd'd'e'e'eWEEP!" and its call a "soft double-noted...che'dip".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Venezuelan bristle tyrant as Near Threatened. It has a small range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. "Although there is still extensive forest cover in parts of its limited range, deforestation has been severe around Caracas, and many other areas have also been degraded ". It is considered fairly common and occurs in at least one national park and probably also in other protected areas.


References

The '''Venezuelan bristle tyrant''' ('''''Pogonotriccus venezuelanus''''') is a [[Near Threatened]] species of [[passerine]] bird in the family [[Tyrannidae]], the tyrant flycatchers. It is [[endemic]] to [[Venezuela]].<ref name=IUCN/><ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/flycatchers/ |title=Tyrant flycatchers |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=August 2024 |access-date=19 August 2024 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Venezuelan bristle tyrant was [[Species description|originally described]] as ''Pogonotriccus venezuelanus''.<ref>{{cite journal |last1=Graf von Berlepsch |first1=Hans |date=1905 |title=Descriptions of New Species and Con-species of Neotropical Birds |url=https://ia800900.us.archive.org/25/items/proceedingsofint04inte/proceedingsofint04inte.pdf |journal=Proceedings of the Fourth International Ornithological Congress |volume=XIV |pages=357–358  |language=Latin, English |access-date=December 5, 2024 }}</ref> Genus ''[[Pogonotriccus]]'' was later merged into genus ''[[Phylloscartes]]''. Beginning in 2016 taxonomic systems resurrected ''Pogonotriccus'' for this species and a few others.<ref>BirdLife International (2016) Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world. Version 9. Available at: http://datazone.birdlife.org/userfiles/file/Species/Taxonomy/BirdLife_Checklist_Version_90.zip [.xls zipped 1 MB].</ref><ref name=Clements2022>Clements, J. F., T. S. Schulenberg, M. J. Iliff, T. A. Fredericks, J. A. Gerbracht, D. Lepage, S. M. Billerman, B. L. Sullivan, and C. L. Wood. 2022. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2022. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/</ref><ref name=SACClist23-05>Remsen, J. V., Jr., J. I. Areta, E. Bonaccorso, S. Claramunt, G. Del-Rio, A. Jaramillo, D. F. Lane, M. B. Robbins, F. G. Stiles, and K. J. Zimmer. Version 31 May 2023. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved June 1, 2023</ref>

The Venezuelan bristle tyrant is [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC14.2/>

==Description==

The Venezuelan bristle tyrant is about {{convert|11.5|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long and weighs {{convert|8|to|9|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a gray crown, yellowish white [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]], and a prominent yellowish white [[eye-ring]] on an otherwise mottled yellowish face. Their ear [[coverts]] have a blackish crescent at the top and rear. Their back and rump are olive. Their wings are blackish with pale yellow edges on the flight feathers. Their wing coverts are blackish with pale yellow tips that show as two [[wing bar]]s. Their tail is olive. Their throat and underparts are bright yellow with a very light olive wash on the breast. Both sexes have a brown iris, a long, pointed, blackish bill with a whitish or pinkish base to the [[mandible]], and gray legs and feet.<ref name=VEBT-BOW>Fitzpatrick, J. W. (2022). Venezuelan Bristle-Tyrant (''Pogonotriccus venezuelanus''), version 1.1. In Birds of the World (N. D. Sly, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.vebtyr2.01.1 retrieved December 5, 2024</ref><ref name=Hilty>{{cite book | last =Hilty | first =Steven L. | title =Birds of Venezuela | publisher =Princeton University Press | edition =second | date =2003 | location =Princeton NJ | page =587 | language =English }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The Venezuelan bristle tyrant has two separate ranges in Venezuela. On is in the [[Venezuelan Coastal Range|coastal mountains]] from [[Carabobo]] through Aragua into the [[Capital District (Venezuela)|Federal District]]. The other is in the interior mountains of southern [[Aragua]] and [[Miranda (state)|Miranda]] states. The species inhabits humid [[montane forest]] where it favors mid-elevation [[cloudforest]] heavy with mosses. In elevation it ranges between {{convert|950|and|1400|m|ft|-2|abbr=on}}.<ref name=VEBT-BOW/><ref name=Hilty/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Venezuelan bristle tyrant is a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=VEBT-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The Venezuelan bristle tyrant feeds on [[arthropod]]s. It forages in the forest's mid-story up into the lower canopy, typically perching upright and making short upward sallies to snatch or hover-glean prey from vegetation. It usually forages singly or in pairs and usually as part of a [[mixed-species feeding flock]].<ref name=VEBT-BOW/><ref name=Hilty/>

===Breeding===

The Venezuelan bristle tyrant is thought to breed between January and April or May. Nothing else is known about the species' breeding biology.<ref name=VEBT-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Pogonotriccus-venezuelanus |species=the Venezuelan bristle tyrant}}

===Vocalization===

The Venezuelan bristle tyrant's song is "a fast trill, ''ch'e'e'e'e'd'd'd'd'd'e'e'eWEEP!''" and its call a "soft double-noted...''che'dip''".<ref name=Hilty/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Venezuelan bristle tyrant as Near Threatened. It has a small range; its population size is not known and is believed to be decreasing. "Although there is still extensive forest cover in parts of its limited range, deforestation has been severe around Caracas, and many other areas have also been degraded ".<ref name=IUCN/> It is considered fairly common<ref name=Hilty/> and occurs in at least one national park and probably also in other protected areas<ref name=VEBT-BOW/><!-- -->.
